content.title: The Great Unification: From i to e^{i pi}
content.subtitle: Nuanced Analysis · IBDP Mathematics AA HL
content.syllabusTopics: Topic 1 (Number & Algebra, HL) · Topic 3 (Geometry & Trigonometry, HL) · Topic 5 (Calculus, HL)
content.prerequisites: Complex Numbers Part 1 & 2, Polynomial Analysis, Quadratics & The Calculus Transition
content.materials: GDC (TI-84 Plus CE or equivalent) and GeoGebra or Desmos. This packet is designed largely without a calculator, in the spirit of Paper 1.
content.compulsoryCore: Parts 0-3 (★ and ★★ questions). Parts 4-8 and extension branches are optional.

commandTerms.definition: A short answer with no working required.
Give a detailed account including reasons or causes.
Reach a conclusion by logical reasoning from results already established.
Obtain a stated result; every logical step must appear.
Establish truth by a rigorous, complete chain of reasoning.
You must use the immediately preceding result. Starting over earns no marks.
A clear diagram showing key features and relative scale; label exact coordinates of intercepts, extrema, and special points.

tokProvocations.body: Euler's identity e^{i pi}+1=0 is routinely voted the most beautiful equation in mathematics. Can aesthetic appeal be evidence of mathematical truth, or is beauty merely a property we project onto results we have already accepted?

The number i was introduced by definition (i^2 = -1) to make unsolvable equations solvable. When it later turned out to describe rotation, alternating current, and quantum states, was i discovered to be real, or did we invent a tool that we then chose to apply? Does usefulness make something real?

internationalMindedness.body: The geometric picture of a complex number was published independently by Caspar Wessel (Norwegian-Danish, 1799) and Jean-Robert Argand (Genevan-French, 1806). The series connecting e, i, and trigonometry were used by Euler (Swiss), but the underlying trigonometry rests on a thousand years of work by Indian (Aryabhata, Madhava of Sangamagrama) and Islamic-world (al-Battani, Abu al-Wafa) mathematicians. Notation is a poor historian.

questions.prompt: Write down the polar form of each, with -pi < theta <= pi: (a) z = 1 + i*sqrt(3)  (b) z = -1  (c) z = -2 - 2i
Describe, in one sentence each, what the two numbers r and theta tell you geometrically about where z sits on the Argand plane.

spotlight.title: Conjecture before rule
spotlight.body: In this part you will discover the multiplication rule for complex numbers before being given it. Work numerically first, then prove the general case.

questions.prompt: Numerical investigation (no formula yet). Using z1 = 2(cos 30 + i*sin 30) and z2 = 3(cos 45 + i*sin 45), compute the product z1*z2 and rewrite it in polar form. Write down its modulus and its argument.
Conjecture. Based on Q3, write a conjecture relating the modulus and argument of a product z1*z2 to the moduli and arguments of z1 and z2.
Demonstrate. Show that your conjecture is correct in general by multiplying the two polar forms and applying the compound-angle formulae.

geometricReading.body: Multiplying by a complex number is a rotation (by its argument) combined with a scaling (by its modulus). Multiplication is a similarity transformation.
